{"step_by_step_thinking": "Waldeyer's ring (or chain) is a circumferential arrangement of lymphoid tissue in the upper aerodigestive tract that serves as a first line of immune defense. According to multiple sources in the MEDCORP documents, the Waldeyer ring is composed of the following components: palatine tonsils, pharyngeal tonsils (adenoids), tubal tonsils, and lingual tonsils. These are all mucosa-associated lymphoid tissues located at the entrance of the respiratory and digestive tracts.

Option A (Palatine tonsils) is explicitly mentioned as a component of Waldeyer's ring in multiple documents (e.g., Document [0], [1], [5], [6], [26]).

Option B (Pharyngeal tonsils) is also clearly identified as part of the ring, located in the roof of the nasopharynx (Document [0], [8], [6]).

Option C (Tubal and lingual tonsils) is included in the description of the ring: tubal tonsils surround the Eustachian tube openings, and lingual tonsils are on the posterior surface of the tongue (Document [0], [8], [5]).

Option D (Post auricular nodes) refers to lymph nodes located behind the ear. These are part of the deep cervical lymphatic chain and are involved in drainage of the head and neck, but they are not components of Waldeyer's ring. The Waldeyer ring consists of lymphoid tissues (tonsils), not lymph nodes. The post auricular nodes are not part of the ring structure.

Therefore, the correct answer is D, as it is the only option that does not form part of Waldeyer's lymphatic chain.", "answer_choice": "D"}
